message:
fix(service/systemd): use restart on re-install so new unit takes effect

`systemctl --user enable --now` is `enable + start`, and `start` on an
already-running service is a no-op. Re-installing (the exact path a
user takes to recover from the #96 crash-loop) wrote a new unit file
with the fixed `WorkingDirectory` + `StartLimit*` directives, but
`enable --now` did NOT restart the running service, so the crashed
process kept spinning against the old unit. The user would still see
the broken state, conclude the fix didn't work, and file another
issue.

Split `enable --now` into explicit `enable` + `restart`. `restart`
starts a not-running service and picks up new unit content on an
already-running one, so it's correct for fresh and re-install alike.
Mirrors the launchd bootout+bootstrap pattern on the macOS path.

Co-Authored-By: Claude Opus 4.7 (1M context) <noreply@anthropic.com>

files.patch:
@@ -23099,12 +23099,17 @@ fn service_install_systemd(
     std::fs::write(&unit_path, &unit_content)?;
     ftui_runtime::ftui_println!("Wrote {}", unit_path.display());
 
-    // Reload and enable+start
+    // Reload, enable, and RESTART. On a re-install (which is exactly how
+    // users will recover from the #96 crash-loop) the old service is still
+    // running with the old unit file; `enable --now` is `enable + start`,
+    // and `start` on a running service is a no-op — so the new unit
+    // content would never actually take effect. `restart` both starts a
+    // not-running service and picks up new unit content on an already-
+    // running one, so it's correct for fresh and re-install alike. This
+    // mirrors the launchd bootout+bootstrap pattern below.
     run_cmd("systemctl", &["--user", "daemon-reload"])?;
-    run_cmd(
-        "systemctl",
-        &["--user", "enable", "--now", SYSTEMD_UNIT_NAME],
-    )?;
+    run_cmd("systemctl", &["--user", "enable", SYSTEMD_UNIT_NAME])?;
+    run_cmd("systemctl", &["--user", "restart", SYSTEMD_UNIT_NAME])?;
 
     ftui_runtime::ftui_println!(
         "Service installed and started (systemd user unit: {})",
